coredata.dc:title: Formation mechanism of carbon dots: From chemical structures to fluorescent behaviors
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Lei
Zan, Minghui
Chen, Fangman
Kou, Xinyue
Liu, Yulu
Wang, Panyong
Mei, Qian
Hou, Zheng
Dong, Wen-Fei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: Despite the numerous reports on carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from o-phenylenediamine (o-pd), the precise mechanism of fluorescence and formation processes of o-pd CDs have not been investigated. In this study, we purified o-pd CDs and obtained five compounds with yellow, green and blue emission. For the first time, two actual molecular fluorophores named 2,3-Diaminophenazine (DAP) and 2-Amino-3-hydroxyphenazine (AHP) were confirmed. More sophisticated investigations showed the DAP was dominant for the yellow emission, which confirmed molecule states in detail. More interestingly, DAP could form nanoparticles (NPs) with various sizes at different concentrations. In this regard, we propose that the formation processes of o-pd CDs were similar to those of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), where DAP structures initially aggregated with themselves to form polymeric clusters, further carbonization of polymeric clusters endowed the polymer clusters with a preliminary core-shell structure. With the collapse of the shell and aromatization of the core, the CPDs like structure formed eventually. This study supplements those on the yellow-emissive fluorophores region and could be the potential reference for the preparation of CDs in a similar system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecular fluorophores
Formation mechanism
LEDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Separation of DAP, AHP, G-CD 1, G-CD 2 and B-CD
Quantum yields of DAP, AHP, G-CD 1, G-CD 2 and B-CD
Fabrication and the character of LEDs
Optical properties and characterizations of the o-pd CDs
G-CD and B-CD separated from the o-pd CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: L. Cao. and M. Zan. contributed equally to this work. This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004600 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21803075 , 91959112 , 81902166 , 62027825 , and 82172077 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, HRTEM and FFT pattern (insets) images of the o-pd CDs. (b) The size distribution of the o-pd CDs. (c) UV–vis absorption, excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of the o-pd CDs. the insets show the o-pd CDs solution under daylight and UV light (λ = 365 nm). (d–f) Fluorescence, FTIR and XPS spectra of the o-pd CDs, respectively.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation spectrum, and emission spectrum of DAP. The insets show the DAP solution under daylight and UV light (λ = 365 nm). (b) Fluorescence spectra of DAP. (c) EDX results, (d) 1H NMR spectrum, and (e) 13C NMR spectrum of DAP.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation spectrum, and emission spectrum of AHP. The insets show the AHP solution under daylight and UV light (λ = 365 nm). (b) The fluorescence spectra of AHP. (c) EDX result, (d) 1H NMR spectrum, and (e) 13C NMR spectrum of AHP.

(a) UV–vis absorptions of G-CD 1, G-CD 2, and B-CD. Fluorescence spectra of (b) G-CD 1, (c) G-CD 2, and (d) B-CD. The insets show the G-CD 1, G-CD 2, and B-CD solutions under daylight and UV light (λ = 365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence decay curves of the o-pd CDs and separated compounds. (b) XRD patterns of AHP and DAP. (c) TEM and HRTEM images and corresponding FFT pattern of DAP. (d) TEM image of AHP. (e) TEM and HRTEM images and corresponding FFT patterns of G-CD 2. (f) TEM image of B-CD.
(a–d) Optical photographs of multi-color LEDs and W-LED. (e–h) CIE color coordinates and (i–l) corresponding emission spectra of blue, green, yellow, and W-LEDs.
Preparation routes and purification of o-pd CDs. Upon the purification, five compounds were separated; two molecular fluorophores, DAP and AHP, were confirmed for the yellow emission.

Proposed formation mechanism of the o-pd CDs, polymeric clusters formed with the aggregation of DAP, further carbonization and collapse led to the formation of CPDs like structure. AHP aggregated with each other under hydrogen-bond interaction and further formed a reticular polymer-like structure.

head.ce:title: Formation mechanism of carbon dots: From chemical structures to fluorescent behaviors

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Life Sciences and Medicine
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite the numerous reports on carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from o-phenylenediamine (o-pd), the precise mechanism of fluorescence and formation processes of o-pd CDs have not been investigated. In this study, we purified o-pd CDs and obtained five compounds with yellow, green and blue emission. For the first time, two actual molecular fluorophores named 2,3-Diaminophenazine (DAP) and 2-Amino-3-hydroxyphenazine (AHP) were confirmed. More sophisticated investigations showed the DAP was dominant for the yellow emission, which confirmed molecule states in detail. More interestingly, DAP could form nanoparticles (NPs) with various sizes at different concentrations. In this regard, we propose that the formation processes of o-pd CDs were similar to those of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), where DAP structures initially aggregated with themselves to form polymeric clusters, further carbonization of polymeric clusters endowed the polymer clusters with a preliminary core-shell structure. With the collapse of the shell and aromatization of the core, the CPDs like structure formed eventually. This study supplements those on the yellow-emissive fluorophores region and could be the potential reference for the preparation of CDs in a similar system.

Two fluorophores corresponded to yellow emission named DAP and AHP were separated from o-pd CDs solution by HPLC for the first time, further investigation suggested these CDs formed from the aggregation of DAP.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: CDs, reported for the first time in 2004 [1], can be divided into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [2], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [3], CPDs [4], and carbon nanodots (CNDs) [5]. CDs have attracted considerable attention because of their unique properties such as high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) [6], tunable emissive wavelength [7], good biocompatibility, and stability against photobleaching [8]. Up to now, there is much literature reported the application in bioimaging [9,10], sensor assays [11], cancer therapy [12], catalyst [13], etc. [14]. However, the actual fluorescence mechanism of CDs is still unclear.

Various hypotheses have been reported on the fluorescence mechanism to explain the fluorescence behavior of CDs, including the quantum confinement effect (QCE) [15], quantum size effect (QSE) [5], surface state [16], crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect [17], and molecule state [18]. The QCE or π-conjugate domain is determined by the carbon core. With the increase in the reaction temperature, the carbon core is formed by dehydration and carbonization of raw molecules and exhibits weak photoluminescence (PL) with a high photostability [19]. Similarly, the QSE or the size of the π-conjugate domain also plays a crucial role in the PL behavior. The band-gap energy is affected by the size of fragments, which decreases with the increase in the size of CDs [20]. Furthermore, the surface state is determined by the connection between the chemical groups and the surface of the carbon backbone. By tuning the reaction conditions such as temperature, reaction time and doping heteroatoms, the chemical groups on the edge of CDs were transformed and eventually led to differences in luminescence performance [16]. The CEE effect was reported for the first time in 2014 by Yang et al. [21], which elucidates the fluorescence mechanism of CPDs, where the carbon core cross-linked to the polymer chains and enhanced the emission by suppressing the rotation and vibration of the PL center or by forming a new emissive structure. In addition to the above hypothesis, the molecule state mechanism has been recently demonstrated [22]. For example, Song et al. successfully isolated a molecular fluorophore 5-oxo-1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo [1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA) from blue-emissive CDs [23] and demonstrated that the main PL center and independent-excitation emission properties were attributed to IPCA. With the increase in reaction temperature, IPCA could convert to a carbon core structure and result in an excitation-dependent emission with a low PLQY. Coincidentally, Soni et al. revealed that quinoxalino [2,3-b]phenazine-2,3-diamine (QXPDA) was the main source of red emission in CDs [22]. Notably, QXPDA exhibited an excitation-independent emission and formed small dots, large dots or ribbon-like structures under different conditions. Thus, it is of interest to evaluate whether CDs are formed by self-aggregation of molecular fluorophores or carbonization of precursors.

In this study, yellow-emissive CDs were prepared by the solvothermal method with o-phenylenediamine (o-pd) and purified via column chromatography. Initially, two precise fluorophore structures, DAP and AHP were confirmed in the o-pd CDs (Scheme 1 ). Two types of green compounds (G-CD, G-CD 1 and G-CD 2) and one type of blue compound (B-CD) were also extracted. Further experiments suggested that DAP was dominant in the fluorescence in o-pd CDs solutions, with a weight accounting for 14.1%. Furthermore, DAP could form nanoparticles (NPs) with various sizes by tuning the concentration. Surprisingly, DAP, AHP, G-CD and B-CD exhibited CPDs, amorphous dots, nanoneedle, graphite sheets and reticulated polymer-like structures. Therefore, we propose that the carbon dots were formed from DAP, where DAP initially aggregated polymeric clusters and subsequently carbonized with increased time. When the reaction proceeded further, the collapse and aromatization process provided the pre-CDs with core-shell-like morphologies. Moreover, considering the high PLQY of these components, multi-color and white-light-emitting diodes (W-LEDs) were successfully prepared, which indicated a high potential for application in the LEDs industry.

O-phenylenediamine (≥99%) was purchased from Adamas Reagent, Ltd. Absolute ethyl alcohol (≥99.5%), dichloromethane (≥99.9%), petroleum ether (60–90 °C), and ethyl acetate (≥99.7%) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Methyl alcohol (≥99.5%), dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, ethylene glycol (EG), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. ET-821 A silica gel, ET-821 B silica gel (the main component is polydimethylsiloxane), and 395 nm UV chips were obtained from the market. Ultrapure water was obtained from Heal Force ultra-pure water system.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of o-pd CDs, DAP, AHP, G-CD and B-CD were obtained from a transmission electron microscope (JEOL-2100F). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS ULTRA DLD). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to study the crystallinity of DAP and AHP (Phillips, The Netherlands). Chromatography and mass spectra were recorded using high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS, Waters Alliance 2695, Waters Micromass ZQ 2000). 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and 13C NMR spectra were obtained by using Varian 400 spectrometers in CD3OD or C2D6SO. The element compositions of DAP and AHP were analyzed using electron-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, JSM-6390LV). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from a Nicolet IS-10 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed using a Zetasizer Nano ZS-90 (Malvern). UV–visible (vis) absorption spectra of the CDs were acquired using a UV–vis absorption spectrometer (Agilent Cary 300 Scan). Fluorescence spectra of the CDs were acquired by an F97 Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence (FL) lifetime was measured by FLS1000 (Edinburgh instrument). Raman spectra were obtained from a Raman spectrometer (Renishaw PLC, England).

Briefly, o-pd CDs were synthesized by a simple one-step solvothermal method using o-pd as a carbon and nitrogen source and absolute ethyl alcohol (≥99.5%) as a solvent. 2 g of o-pd was dissolved in 20 mL absolute ethyl alcohol. After an ultrasonic treatment for 15 min, until the mixture became clear, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Dark brown products were obtained when the autoclave cooled to room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of DAP, AHP, G-CD 1, G-CD 2 and B-CD
ce:section.ce:para: The dark brown solution was mixed with silica gel and dried using a rotary evaporator. DAP and AHP were separated from o-pd CDs by column chromatography using dichloromethane (DCM) and methanol as eluent. The ratios of DCM to methanol were 20:1 and 40:1. After elution, the yellow-compounds solution was dried by a freezer dryer and stored in the dark at −20 °C for future use.

Conventional methods were also used for purification. Briefly, the dark brown solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min to remove the precipitate. The supernatant was then dialyzed in ultrapure water (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) = 1000 Da) for 48 h. The fluid outside the dialysis system was collected and separated by chromatography using DCM and methanol (20:1) as eluent. The sample was dried by a freezer dryer and stored in the dark at −20 °C.

G-CD 1 and G-CD 2 were obtained from o-pd CDs by column chromatography, but the eluent was petroleum ether (PE) and ethyl acetate (EtOAc). The ratio of PE to EtOAc was 10:1. After elution, the green-compounds solution was dried using rotary evaporators and stored in the dark at −20 °C.

The B-CD was separated from o-pd CDs using high-performance thin-layer chromatography (HPTLC). PE and EtOAc were used as developing solvents. The ratio of PE to EtOAc was 5:1. The blue-emissive parts were gently scraped off with a scraper, while the B-CD was completely separated. The silica gel mixed with the blue-emissive parts was then soaked in methanol for sonication. Subsequently, the methanol was removed by rotary evaporators and stored in the dark at −20 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yields of DAP, AHP, G-CD 1, G-CD 2 and B-CD
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yields (QYs) of DAP, AHP, G-CD 1, G-CD 2, and B-CD were measured by a reported formula [24], based on rhodamine 6G dissolved in absolute ethanol or quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 as a reference [25]. The QYs were calculated by the reported equation as follows:
Q s
=
Q r
Δ
I s
I r
Δ
A r
A s
Δ
(
ŋ r
ŋ s
)
2
where Q is the quantum yield, I and A are the integral of fluorescence intensity and UV–vis absorption, respectively, and ŋ represents the refractive index. “s” represents the samples including DAP, AHP, G-CD 1, G-CD 2 and B-CD, while “r” refers to the references include rhodamine 6G and quinine sulfate. The absorptions of DAP, AHP, G-CD 1, G-CD 2, B-CD, rhodamine 6G, and quinine sulfate were limited to below 0.10 to avoid self-absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and the character of LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: All types of LEDs were obtained by similar fabrication processes [26,27]. The emission peak of the UV chips (obtained from the market) was at 395 nm at an operating voltage of 3.0 V. Multi-color LEDs were prepared by adding 20 mg DAP, G-CD and B-CD into 30 mL of ethanol. 2g of PVP was added and mixed ultrasonically for 30 min to prepare different phosphors. The CD/PVP powders were obtained after drying in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, 2 g of the CD/PVP powder was homogeneously mixed with 1.6 g of the ET-821 A silica gel and 0.4 g of the ET-821 B silica gel and then coated on 395 nm UV chips. W-LEDs were manipulated by mixing CDs with an appropriate weight ratio (DAP: G-CD: B-CD = 3: 2: 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and characterizations of the o-pd CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, o-pd CDs were synthesized by the solvothermal treatment of the mixture with o-pd and absolute ethanol. A dark brown solution was obtained when the autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature. It was then centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 20 min to remove the large precipitate. The supernatant was used without further purification. The morphologies of the o-pd CDs were elucidated by TEM, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the o-pd CDs had a distinct crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and an average diameter of approximately 6.62 nm. The optical properties were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer and fluorophotometer. In Fig. 1c, the UV–vis absorption band at 236 nm is attributed to the n-σ∗ transition of C-NH or C-OH while the absorption band at 293 nm is assigned to the π-π∗ transition of the conjugated phenazine ring included C=C and C=N. The peak at 421 nm is most likely attributed to the conjugated structure between phenazine ring structure and –NH2 group [28]. The fluorescence spectra of the o-pd CDs (Fig. 1c and d) show that the maximum emission is located at 538 nm with the maximum excitation at 450 nm. Consistent with the reported literature [29], the o-pd CDs solution exhibited an excitation-independent emission. The molecule structure, chemical bonds and functional groups were investigated by FTIR spectroscopy and XPS. As depicted in Fig. 1e, the absorption peaks at 3386 and 3189 cm−1 suggest N-H and O-H groups. And the peaks at 3038 and 1633 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of C-H and C=N, respectively. The absorption band at 1590 cm−1 originates from the bending vibration of N-H, while the absorption at 1501 cm−1 corresponded to the structure C=C. The stretching vibration of C-N is located at 1273 cm−1. XPS was performed to further analyze the chemical bonds of the o-pd CDs. As shown in Fig. 1f, the o-pd CDs consisted of three main elements: C, N and O with contents of 68.25%, 18.88% and 12.87%, respectively. High-resolution XPS spectra are presented in Fig. S1. The result of C 1s XPS spectrum reflects three types of carbon bonds in the o-pd CDs, including C=C/C-C (284.8 eV), C-N/C-O (285.9 eV) and C=N/C=O (287.8 eV). The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.9 eV (graphite N), 399.1 eV (amino N) and 401.4 eV (pyridine N). The two peaks at 532.8 and 532.0 eV are attributed to the structures of C=O and C-O, respectively. Therefore, the results of UV–vis, FTIR and XPS suggested the presence of phenazine structure in the o-pd CDs.

Considering the high degree of similarity of fluorescence properties of o-pd CDs and DAP, we speculated DAP formed in the preparation of CDs and had the largest contribution to the fluorescence. For purification, DCM and methanol were used as eluents in column chromatography. The major yellow-emissive component separated from o-pd CDs was analyzed by EDX spectroscopy, electrospray ionization (ESI) MS, NMR and FTIR spectroscopy. And the presence of the molecular fluorophore DAP (C12H10N4, M = 210.24) was confirmed. Beyond our expectation, the o-pd CDs solution without treatment contained approximately 14.1-wt% DAP, and only 1.8-wt% DAP was acquired in the supernatant after centrifugation. Thus, the o-pd CDs contained a supersaturated concentration of DAP, as a large number of products were formed during the preparation of CDs.

The optical properties and chemical characterization of the fluorophore DAP were carried out and shown in Fig. 2 a, the UV–vis absorption peaks at 207 and 261 nm originate from contributions of the n-σ∗ transition of C-NH and conjugate structure of C=C/C=N, which caused the π-π∗ transition. However, the absorption band at 432 nm indicates the electron transition of the conjugated structure between the phenazine ring structure and –NH2. The fluorescence spectra in Fig. 2b indicate DAP had a bright yellow emission at 537 nm when excited by a 450 nm optical source and exhibited an excitation-independent emission, very close to that of the o-pd CDs. An EDX analysis was performed to further investigate the elemental composition of DAP. According to Fig. 2c, the result of EDX suggests DAP consists of carbon (71.61%) and nitrogen (28.39%) elements. As shown in Fig. S2, the mass spectrum had a strong peak at m/z = 211.3. This mass-charge ratio was attributed to (C12H10N4+H)+. Weak peaks at m/z = 212.4 and m/z = 249.5 corresponded to (C12H10N4+2H)2+ and (C12H10N4+K)+, respectively. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra are shown in Fig. 2d and e, respectively, to illustrate the chemical structure of DAP. The NMR data of DAP are presented in Scheme S1. Moreover, the FTIR spectrum of DAP is shown in Fig. S3a. As expected, the FTIR and NMR results are identical to the reported results [30]. Besides that, XPS was used to further investigate the chemical bonds of DAP. The XPS spectrum in Fig. S3b shows that DAP consists of three elements including C, N and O. For this phenomenon, the powder of DAP might absorb oxygen gas, which yielded the peaks of oxygen in the XPS spectrum. The results of the high-resolution C 1s spectrum are shown in Fig. S3c, with three peaks at 284.8, 285.8 and 287.2 eV, attributed to chemical bonds of C=C/C-C, C-N and C=N, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum in Fig. S3d shows two peaks at 399.0 eV and 399.8 eV, which correspond to the molecular structures of graphite N and amino N. Because the EDX, ESI-MS, NMR and FTIR data are of significance for a precise determination of the molecular structure. And the obtained results are consistent with the literature on DAP [30,31]. Thus, we have enough evidence to suggest that a large number of Y-CD obtained from o-pd CDs is the fluorophore DAP.

To the best of our knowledge, AHP (C12H9N3O, M = 211.22) was accompanied by the hydrolytic action of amidogen when DAP was synthesized by the oxidation of o-pd in the FeCl3/HCl system [32]. Hence, whether the o-pd CDs solution contained not only DAP but also AHP? Therefore, by tuning the ratio of DCM and methanol, a small amount of AHP was successfully separated from the o-pd CDs. The optical properties and chemical structure were analyzed in the same manner as that for DAP. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the UV–vis absorption indicated three peaks at 209, 264 and 433 nm, attributed to the n-σ∗ electron transition of C-NH of AHP, conjugated structure of C=C/C=N for the π-π∗ transition in the phenazine ring, and electron transition of the conjugated structure between the phenazine ring structure and –NH2, respectively. Fluorescence spectra are depicted in Fig. 3a and b. In accordance with DAP and AHP exhibit yellow fluorescence with an excitation-independent emission, with the maximum emission at 538 nm when the excitation wavelength was 450 nm. The EDX results of AHP (Fig. 3c) suggest that the contents of C, N and O elements are 74.9%, 17.6% and 7.5%, respectively. The ESI-MS spectra of AHP are presented in Fig. S4, the mass spectrum (ES+) showed a peak at m/z = 212.4, which indicates the structure of (C12H9N3O + H)+. The peak at m/z = 210.4 in the ESI spectrum corresponds to (C12H9N3O-H)-. Thus, the mass spectrum is consistent with the relative molecular mass of AHP. Subsequently, NMR spectroscopy was performed to further elucidate the chemical structure of AHP. The 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were shown in Fig. 3d and e, respectively; detailed data are presented in Scheme S2. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum of AHP is shown in Fig. S5a. Compared to the previous study [33], the second molecular fluorophore AHP was confirmed by EDX, ESI-MS, NMR, and FTIR spectroscopy. The XPS spectra of AHP are shown in Figs. S5b–e. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum suggests four different states of carbon including C-C (284.1 eV), C=C (284.9 eV), C-O (285.7 eV) and C=O (288.5 eV). And the high-resolution N 1s spectrum demonstrates the presence of amino N (398.2 eV) and graphite N (400.0 eV). In the high-resolution O 1s spectrum, two peaks at 531.7 and 532.9 eV correspond to C-O and C=O, respectively. The existence of C=O may account for the deprotonation of the C-OH in AHP.

However, we calculated the yield of AHP in o-pd CDs solution; it was below 0.2%. This result may be ascribed to the hydrolysis condition of DAP, which has the best hydrolysis effect under acidic conditions. In this regard, the reaction mechanism and synthesis routes of DAP and AHP are shown in Scheme S3. However, regardless of the contents of DAP and AHP in o-pd CDs, this work is the first to separate two yellow-emissive molecular fluorophores with accurate structures from the o-pd CDs solution and fill the vacant field of yellow emissive molecular state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: G-CD and B-CD separated from the o-pd CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to DAP and AHP, two types of G-CD and one type of B-CD responsible for green and blue emissions, respectively, were also separated. In contrast to DAP and AHP, G-CD 1 and G-CD 2 were eluted with an appropriate ratio of PE and EtOAc. As shown in Fig. S6, G-CD 1 and G-CD 2 could be easily observed in the column under blue-light (λ = 450 nm). However, the UV–vis absorption (Fig. 4 a) of G-CD 1 and G-CD 2 almost overlapped with each other. And the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 4b and c shows that both G-CD 1 and G-CD 2 exhibit excitation-independent emission with bright green fluorescence. Their maximum excitation wavelengths are approximately 410 nm. Notably, the positions of the emissive peaks of G-CD 1 (454, 483, 516 nm) are in line with those of G-CD 2 (457, 483, 511 nm). Recently, an analogous case has been reported [34]. Vanessa et al. purified CDs by HPLC and obtained deprotonated and protonated fluorophores whose UV–vis absorption and fluorescence characteristics almost coincided. Thus, we assumed that G-CD 1 and G-CD 2 are unknown fluorescent molecules and their protonated state.

While, B-CD was not enough to separate by column chromatography, for this case, high-performance thin-layer chromatography (HPTLC) was used to address this issue. The UV–vis absorption of B-CD (Fig. 4a) shows an inconspicuous peak at 325 nm, which may be assigned to the conjugate structure of C=C or C=N. As shown in Fig. 4d, B-CD exhibits an almost excitation-independent emission. The maximum emissive position is at 445 nm with a negligible shift with the increase in the wavelength of exciting light. The inset in Fig. 4d suggested that the B-CD solution appeared transparent under daylight and bright blue fluorescence under UV light (λ = 365 nm).

Although the components of green and blue emissions were obtained, the precise structures were not confirmed because of the complex composition. However, based on these experiments, we speculated that G-CD 1 and G-CD 2 originated from an uncharted fluorophore with its protonated state, while B-CD was assumed to be a derivative of quinoxaline. All above compounds exhibited excitation-independent emissions, which indicates that the o-pd CDs had different PL characteristics owing to the different molecular state.

To explore the formation mechanism of o-pd CDs, the common purification method of dialysis was employed. For the o-pd CDs, a visible fluorescence was observed from the solution outside the dialysis bag (MW = 1000 Da) during the dialytic process. It might be ascribed to DAP, which escaped from the dialysis bag and exhibited fluorescence in the dialysate. Therefore, we investigated the difference between primary o-pd CDs solution and dialysate. As shown in Fig. S7, the TLC result revealed that both dialysate and primary o-pd CDs solution contained large amounts of DAP. The MS and NMR (Fig. S8) results of dialysate further confirmed this conclusion. Nevertheless, other components were not observed from the dialysate because of the low content. Additionally, the fluorescence spectra of the o-pd CDs in Fig. 1d do not show the feature peaks of G-CD and B-CD. Thus, we believe that, first, the three components in the o-pd CDs solution were too rare to be detected and, second, they may correspond to the large overlaps between the excitation spectra of DAP and AHP with the emission spectra of G-CD and B-CD, which caused the self-absorption and energy transduction and only reflected the yellow emission.

The solvent effect on o-pd CDs, DAP and AHP were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S9, the maximum emission peaks positions of the o-pd CDs gradually red-shifted from 535 to 565 nm in protic solvents with the increase in polarity, with similar solvent-dependent emission to those of DAP and AHP (Table S1). However, the solvent effect of aprotic solvents was complex and could be affected by hydrogen bonds, internal charge transfer, probe-probe interactions, and conformational changes [35]. They eventually resulted in differences in the solvent-dependent emission. Furthermore, in contrast to AHP, the fluorescence spectra of DAP in different solvents had high degrees of similarity to the o-pd CDs, which also demonstrates that DAP is dominant in the fluorescence in the o-pd CDs. Moreover, both o-pd CDs, DAP and AHP could be effectively quenched by water, owing to the relatively low solubility in water [29]. Except that, the concentration effect of DAP in the range of 10−3 to 10 mM was also studied. As shown in Fig. S10, the maximum emission peak red-shifted from 537 to 586 nm with the increase in concentration. A slight excitation-dependent emission emerged when the concentration of DAP reached 2 mM. The corresponding excitation spectra are shown in Figs. S11a and b, where the maximum excitation is initially blue-shifted, and then red-shifted with the increase in content, which implies the various emissive states. To investigate the feasibility of forming NPs by DAP, DLS was employed to monitor the average diameter of DAP dissolved in ethanol, allowed to stand for 48 h. As shown in Fig. S12, the average diameters enlarged from 2.73 to 45.33 nm in the concentration range of 10−3 to 10 mM. The corresponding UV–vis absorptions which are shown in Fig. S11c also confirm the formation of NPs. The results indicate that the aggregated structure formed when the concentration of DAP reached 0.5 mM. The corresponding emission spectra (Fig. S10) also demonstrate the result, because the red-shifted emission was observed when the concentration of DAP reached 0.5 mM. This implied that DAP could form NPs with different sizes under the stacking effect, as reported [36].

The NMR results of the primary o-pd CDs in Fig. S13 indicate that the o-pd CDs contained the fluorophore DAP and considerable content of the raw material o-pd [37]. However, o-pd is a photosensitizer that is easily oxidized by light irradiation [38] or oxidizing agents [39]. As shown in Fig. S14a, we detected the fluorescence of o-pd dissolved in ethanol under irradiation by sunlight for 0 and 4 days. Compared to the blank control, the fluorescence of o-pd was enhanced 37 times. The result of TLC (Figs. S14b and c) indicates that the fluorescence enhancement was due to the formation of DAP. Furthermore, the fluorescence stabilities of the o-pd CDs, DAP and AHP were also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S15a, the o-pd CDs, DAP and AHP have good stabilities at different concentrations of NaCl (0–200 mM). The effects of the pH and irradiation time on fluorescence were also investigated. According to Figs. S15b–i, compared to DAP and AHP, the o-pd CDs had good stability in the pH range of 4–12 and considerably better photostability after irradiation with blue light (λ = 450 nm, P = 10 W) for 60 min. This suggests that the o-pd CDs solution was in a dynamic equilibrium, in which consumed DAP was supplemented by supersaturated DAP or oxidation of o-pd, eventually providing the o-pd CDs solution with an excellent photobleaching resistance. The fluorescence decay curves of the o-pd CDs and compounds separated from them are shown in Fig. 5 a, the lifetime of the o-pd CDs, DAP, AHP, G-CD 1, and G-CD 2 was calculated to be approximately 4.03, 4.64, 3.51, 4.03, and 1.50 ns with a single-exponential decay tendency (Table S2), which indicates that the emission originated from the simple fluorescent center [23]. The abnormal behavior of the o-pd CDs was attributed to the too high content of DAP to coverage other fluorophore signals. However, the lifetime of B-CD was ca. 2.30 ns. Its double-exponential fluorescence intensity decay tendency implies several emissive states. The QYs of DAP, AHP, G-CD 1, G-CD 2 and B-CD were approximately 15.35%, 8.75%, 9.86%, 14.67% and 14.54%, respectively.

The XRD patterns of DAP and AHP are shown in Fig. 5b. In addition to the identical peaks of DAP, the sharp peak at 10.26° corresponds to the (001) peak with a calculated interlayer spacing of 0.86 nm. The weak peak at 39.42° corresponds to the (100) peak of graphite carbon with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which is consistent with the crystalline structure and FFT pattern in Fig. 5c. However, the XRD results of AHP do not show the above peaks, which were verified by the reticulated polymer-like structure in Fig. 5d. Furthermore, the TEM images of G-CD 1 (Fig. S16) show a nano-needle-like structure (Figs. S16a and b), which may be attributed to the low solubility because visible crystallites in ethanol are observed. A graphene-sheet-like structure (Figs. S16c and d) with nanodot-like NPs on their edge was also observed. The nanodots like NPs (Figs. S16e and f) exhibit an amorphous morphology with an average diameter of 7 nm. Moreover, the TEM images of G-CD 2 and B-CD are presented in Fig. 5e and f, respectively. G-CD 2 exhibits a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm with a dot-like structure, while B-CD exhibits an amorphous-dot-like configuration. This is the first study demonstrating that CDs contained both CPDs, graphene sheet, amorphous dots and reticulated polymer-like structures with PL properties.

As we know, the preparation of CDs via hydrothermal reaction was complicated and a cup of cocktail with various substances was obtained after the reaction. Because the over-saturation concentration of DAP was obtained in the o-pd CDs and the oxidized production of o-pd by sunlight was DAP, we speculated that the formation of o-pd CDs originated from an over-saturation concentration of DAP. As shown in Scheme 2 , a large amount of DAP was initially produced when o-pd was oxidized and cyclized under hydrothermal conditions. As DAP was dominant for the yellow emission, it could be regarded as a monomer responsible for the fluorescence. With time, the DAP structures began to aggregate with each other in a horizontal or vertical plane [40]. The hydrogen bond interaction between DAP molecules and the π-π stacking effect primarily caused polymeric clusters of DAP growth. Then, the carbonization of DAP polymeric clusters, accompanied by the formation of C-N bonds in polymer fragments, eventually led to rudiment carbon cores with numerous polymer chains on the surface. Subsequently, the polymer chains gradually shrunk toward the carbon core to form a preliminary shell structure and ulteriorly collapsed with the aromatization of the carbon core. Then, the carbon dots were completely formed, as shown in Fig. 5c. This case was similar to the formation process of CPDs [4,23,41]. The HRTEM image of DAP NPs in Fig. S17a confirms our claim. The crystalline region inside the white circle indicates a carbon core structure, while the non-crystalline region between the red circle and white circle is attributed to the shell structure [42]. This suggests that it was a para-crystalline carbon core structure composed of tiny carbon clusters surrounded by polymer fragments. Moreover, both o-pd CDs, DAP and AHP show the presence of D-band (∼1340-1345 cm−1) and the G-band (∼1560-1580 cm−1) in Raman spectra (Fig. S17b). The ratio of ID/IG is an important figure of merit related to the defect density and the crystallite size [43,44]. In our experiment, the ratios of ID and IG are calculated to be approximately 1.40, 3.15, and 2.62, respectively. As we know, D-band is activated by the presence of defects, molecular fluorophores DAP and AHP were more disordered than o-pd CDs. The o-pd CDs contained a lower value of ID/IG because of the aromatization of the carbon core, which confirmed our guess.

The relationship between DAP and CDs was further investigated. The fluorescence of the o-pd CDs dialyzed for different days was monitored. As shown in Fig. S18, the intensity of the solution in the dialysis bag dramatically decreased, as excepted, and the emission transformed from excitation-independent to excitation-dependent, which demonstrates the different emissive states of the carbon core [45]. This implies that isolated DAP may be attached to the shell outside of the carbon core, provided the CDs with a high PLQY, and dissociate when the CDs solution was diluted. Therefore, DAP covers the emission of the carbon core and exhibits only its fluorescent properties. However, AHP was slightly different from DAP, the AHP structures also aggregated with each other in the initial stage. With the increase in the reaction time, the polymeric clusters of AHP led to further aggregation because of the hydrogen bond interaction between the functional groups of N-H and O-H in AHP. Eventually, a reticular polymer-like structure was formed, as shown in Fig. 5d. In this case, it might be attributed to the hydrogen bond interaction in AHP between N-H and O-H being stronger than that of N-H with N-H in DAP, and thus AHP forms a large reticular polymeric structure.

Finally, we evaluated the potential application of multi-color LEDs and high color rendering index (CRI) W-LEDs. DAP, G-CD, and B-CD were dropped into a PVP powder and mixed with ethanol. After drying in a rotary evaporator, CD/PVP powders were obtained and encapsulated with polydimethylsiloxane. To prepare blue, green and yellow LEDs, the UV chips were coated with encapsulated compounds containing DAP, G-CD, and B-CD, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , the Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates are (0.15, 0.08), (0.26, 0.41) and (0.51, 0.44), respectively. The W-LED was fabricated by tuning DAP/PVP, G-CD/PVP and B-CD/PVP at an appropriate ratio (Fig. 6d, h and l). The CIE coordinates and correlated color temperature of the prepared W-LED are (0.38, 0.42) and 4332 K, respectively. The CRI was as high as 87. This indicates the prepared W-LED possessed high color rendering ability and application potential in the LEDs industry.

In summary, the yellow-emissive CDs with an excitation-independent emission were prepared by solvothermal of o-pd. Through further purification, molecular fluorophores DAP and AHP were confirmed for the first time, which were dominant in the yellow emission. Components with excitation-independent behaviors for green and blue emission were also separated. Further experiments demonstrated that DAP had a fluorescence accordant with that of the o-pd CDs in different solvents, while DAP could form NPs with various sizes with the increase in concentration. The TEM images of the five compounds separated from the o-pd CDs showed different morphologies, including CPDs, nanoneedles, graphite sheets, amorphous dot structures. The formation of o-pd CDs could be attributed to the aggregation, carbonization and collapse of DAP. The attached DAP and isolated DAP could be responsible for the fluorescence properties of the o-pd CDs. Finally, DAP, G-CD and B-CD separated from o-pd CDs were applied to W-LED with good optical properties. Our work expands the study of the molecular state in the yellow-emissive region, which can guide the preparation of CDs in a similar system. We further explored a potential application in the W-LED industry by fluorophores.

Lei Cao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Minghui Zan: Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Fangman Chen: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Xinyue Kou: Software, Data curation. Yulu Liu: Formal analysis, structure of the materials. Panyong Wang: Formal analysis. Qian Mei: Methodology, Data curation. Zheng Hou: Methodology, Data curation. Wen-Fei Dong: Methodology, Data curation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Li Li: Supervision, Software, Data curation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Dervishi, Z. Ji, H. Htoon, M. Sykora, S.K. Doorn, Raman spectroscopy of bottom-up synthesized graphene quantum dots: size and structure dependence, Nanoscale 11(35) (2019) 16571-16581.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Raman spectroscopy as a versatile tool for studying the properties of graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.C. Ferrari, D.M. Basko, Raman spectroscopy as a versatile tool for studying the properties of graphene, Nature Nanotechnology 8(4) (2013) 235-246.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Resolving the multiple emission centers in carbon dots: from fluorophore molecular states to aromatic domain states and carbon-core states

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shamsipur, A. Barati, A.A. Taherpour, M. Jamshidi, Resolving the Multiple Emission Centers in Carbon Dots: From Fluorophore Molecular States to Aromatic Domain States and Carbon-Core States, The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters 9(15) (2018) 4189-4198.
